one_line_takeaway: Malicious OpenClaw skills can hijack an autonomous coding agent purely through benign-looking natural-language bootstrap guidance -- no explicit malicious code -- yielding credential theft, backdoors, and file destruction at 16-64% success while 94% of the skills slip past static, LLM-based, and commercial scanners.
key_finding: Guidance injection is broadly effective and highly stealthy. Against an uninjected DeepSeek-V3 baseline, attack success is 0% for Information Gathering, Privilege Escalation, and Supply Chain, and only 0.5/0.22/0.12 for Persistence/Data-Leak/Disruption, confirming that post-injection gains are attributable to the injected guidance. After injection, per-model per-category success varies widely: DeepSeek-V3.2 is most vulnerable (e.g., Supply Chain 1.00, Disruption 0.69, Persistence 0.67), Kimi K2.5 second (Info Gathering 1.00, Supply Chain 1.00), while Claude Opus 4.6 is most resistant (success essentially only in Supply Chain, ~0.50; Persistence ~0.19; Info Gathering ~0.06) and Gemini 3.1 Pro is most resistant overall. Supply-chain / 'optimization'-framed attacks succeed against nearly all models. On stealth, 94% of skills evade detection: Static Pattern Scan flags 1/10 high-risk (10%) and 0% medium/low; LLM-Guard flags 0/26; the Hybrid Classifier flags 1/10 high-risk; and commercial SkillScan assigns high-risk skills only an average 19.75/100 (medium 14.70, low 14.88), versus 100/100 for an overtly-modifying official skill. Headline: attacks succeed at rates the abstract reports as 16.0%-64.2% across models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Guidance Injection,' a new attack vector against OpenClaw autonomous coding agents. Rather than embedding explicit malicious commands (traditional prompt/skill injection), a malicious third-party skill uses the agent:bootstrap lifecycle hook to inject adversarial natural-language guidance files (e.g., SOUL.md, GUIDE.md) into the agent's initialization/reasoning context. These 'operational narratives' redefine what the agent considers routine best practice, so when the user later issues an ambiguous request (e.g., 'clean up disk space'), the agent autonomously performs harmful actions (deleting .git dirs, exfiltrating credentials, installing backdoors) while believing it is being helpful. Concretely, the authors' OWN contributions are: (1) formulating and characterizing guidance injection with four manipulation strategies (Authority Implantation, Goal Misgeneralization, Distributed Concealment, Autonomy Encouragement); (2) an iterative 'dual-role' adversarial skill-generation pipeline (an Agent-Simulator role + a Skill-Vetter role, implemented with Claude Sonnet) that co-evolves skills to be effective yet evade review; (3) a set of 26 malicious skills spanning 13 attack categories over 3 risk tiers; and (4) ORE-Bench, a reproducible simulated-developer-workspace benchmark. Note: the widely-cited statistic that 26.1% of 31,132 real-world skills contain >=1 vulnerability is from prior work (Liu et al., ref [15]), NOT this paper's measurement.
threat_model: Attacker = a malicious skill developer who publishes a seemingly-benign OpenClaw skill through public channels/marketplaces (ClawHub) where users obtain extensions. The attacker fully controls the skill's implementation, its lifecycle hooks, and its bootstrap guidance files, and can register the agent:bootstrap hook to inject arbitrary natural-language guidance into the agent's initialization context. The attacker CANNOT modify the OpenClaw core framework or the underlying LLM, and cannot directly execute code on the victim host without the agent's cooperation. The attack is enabled by three OpenClaw architectural properties: access to private data (credential stores, config dirs beyond the project workspace), exposure to untrusted content, and autonomous execution capability (the agent resolves ambiguity by acting rather than asking). Defender is assumed to use existing skill-ecosystem defenses only: static keyword/command scanners, LLM-based semantic analyzers, and manual review; NO strict sandboxing or capability isolation is assumed, reflecting OpenClaw's default deployment.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) ORE-Bench cannot capture the full diversity of real developer setups, which may shift success rates; (2) only six LLM backends tested; newer safety-trained models may differ; (3) attacks are specific to OpenClaw's bootstrap mechanism and may not transfer to other agent frameworks; (4) only the skill-hook injection channel is studied (web-search / documentation-ingestion channels unexplored). Reviewer-observed grounding concerns: the paper contains material internal inconsistencies that undercut its headline numbers -- the number of prompts is variously stated as 52 (abstract/conclusion), 78 (intro), and 26 (Appendix C note, 'one per skill', though the setup says 2 per skill = 52); the peak success rate is '64.2%' (abstract) vs 'up to 89%' (intro); the benchmark is called both 'ORE-Bench' and 'DevSecBench'; and the persona is 'Alex Wang' (Sec 5) but the home path/user is '/home/faii' (Appendix). Success is judged partly manually and partly by an LLM judge (DeepSeek-V3), introducing subjectivity. 'OpenClaw' and 'ClawHub' appear to be pseudonymized/fictionalized platform names (all cited docs point to openclaw.ai), so real-world applicability to a specific named product cannot be verified from the text.

datasets_used: ORE-Bench (OpenClaw Realistic Environment Benchmark) -- synthetic developer home directory (persona 'Alex Wang') with configuration, credential, project (5 projects), certificate, logging/notes, and honeypot layers; all secrets synthetic; also referred to as 'DevSecBench' in Secs 6/8
26 authored malicious OpenClaw skills across 13 attack categories and 3 risk tiers (High n=10, Medium n=8, Low n=8)
52 trigger prompts (2 underspecified natural developer prompts per skill; count reported inconsistently as 52/78/26 across the paper)
ClawHub marketplace analysis identifying 134 popular skills that use lifecycle hooks (top skill 'self-improving-agent' = 221,000 downloads)
quantitative_results: Overall attack success rate (abstract): 16.0%-64.2% across six LLM backends; intro states 'up to 89%' (internal inconsistency)
Test matrix: 26 skills x 6 models x 2 prompts = 312 manually-analyzed runs
Uninjected DeepSeek-V3 baseline success: Information Gathering 0, Privilege Escalation 0, Supply Chain 0; Persistence 0.50, Data Leakage 0.22, Disruption 0.12
DeepSeek-V3.2 post-injection (most vulnerable): Info Gather 0.50, Priv Esc 0.50, Persistence 0.67, Data Leak 0.50, Disruption 0.69, Supply Chain 1.00
Kimi K2.5: Info Gather 1.00, Priv Esc 0.25, Persistence 0.50, Data Leak 0.44, Disruption 0.12, Supply Chain 1.00
GPT-5.3-Codex: Info Gather 0.25, Priv Esc 0.50, Persistence 0.67, Data Leak 0.28, Disruption 0.44, Supply Chain 0.50

Qwen3 Max: five radar values recovered (0.25, 0.33, 0.39, 0.50, 1.00) but exact category mapping is not recoverable from the flattened plot; per Sec 6.4.3 Qwen shows scattered success across categories

Gemini 3.1 Pro (most resistant overall): five radar values recovered (0.50, 0.33, 0.33, 0.38, 0.50); per Sec 6.4.3 Gemini is selectively vulnerable to data leakage and supply chain (exact spoke labels not recoverable from OCR)
Claude Opus 4.6 (most resistant): only three radar values recovered (0.06, 0.19, 0.50); per Sec 6.4.3 prose, Claude's success is essentially only in Supply Chain attacks
Stealth / evasion: 94% of the 26 skills evade detection overall
Static Pattern Scan detection: 10% (1/10) high-risk, 0% (0/8) medium, 0% (0/8) low
Hybrid Classifier detection: 10% (1/10) high, 0% medium, 0% low
SkillScan average risk score (0-100): high-risk 19.75, medium 14.70, low 14.88; for reference an overt official skill scored 100
baselines_compared: Uninjected DeepSeek-V3 (no guidance injection) as the clean attack-success baseline
Six LLM backends compared against each other: Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.3-Codex, Qwen3 Max, Kimi K2.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2
Detection/defense baselines for stealth: custom Static Pattern Scan, LLM-Guard (Protect AI, 10 input scanners), a Claude-based Hybrid Classifier, and the commercial SkillScan vetting platform
